title: Harissa chicken
subtitle: Chicken thighs marinated in harissa and lemon, roasted until charred at the edges
excerpt: Bone-in chicken thighs coated in a marinade of harissa, lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil, then roasted at high heat until the outside is charred at the edges and the meat is cooked through. Harissa does most of the flavour work. The marinade needs at least 30 minutes and benefits from overnight.
sourceNotes: Harissa is documented in Tunisian cookery literature from the thirteenth century. The combination of harissa and chicken is a staple of North African and Levantine home cooking. This oven-roasted preparation is a domestic adaptation of the grilled and spit-roasted versions common in Tunisian street food.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate overnight in the fridge for a deeper flavour. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before roasting.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan 200°C; use the upper half of the oven

content.text: Harissa is a North African chilli paste made from dried red chillies, garlic, caraway, and coriander. Different brands vary significantly in heat level — rose harissa is milder and slightly sweeter; standard harissa is hot. Both work here; adjust the quantity to your preference. The high oven temperature is necessary to get the charring on the skin that replicates what a grill or spit would produce.

Serve with flatbreads and yoghurt to offset the heat, or with roasted vegetables alongside. The pan juices, which will be quite dark and intensely flavoured, are worth spooning over the finished chicken.

Combine the {{harissa}} of harissa, lemon juice from the {{lemon}} lemon, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs and turn to coat thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes, or overnight.

Heat the oven to 220°C (fan 200°C). Arrange the chicken skin-side up in a single layer in a roasting pan — do not crowd them. Roast for 35 to 40 minutes until the skin is charred at the edges and crisp, and the juices run clear when the meat is pierced at the thickest point. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with {{greek-yoghurt}} Greek yoghurt and {{coriander}} of coriander if using.

items.symptom: Chicken is cooked but not charred
items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or too low in the oven
items.fix: Use the upper half of the oven; increase to 240°C for the final 5 minutes if needed, or finish under the grill

items.symptom: Harissa is burning before the chicken is cooked
items.cause: Oven temperature too high or chicken piled rather than spread in a single layer
items.fix: Spread thighs in a single layer with space between them; if the marinade is darkening too fast, loosely tent with foil

items.symptom: Flavour is mild despite generous harissa
items.cause: Marinade time too short, or harissa brand is very mild
items.fix: Marinate overnight; use a hotter harissa; score the chicken thighs before marinating to help the paste penetrate

content.text: Harissa originated in Tunisia, where it is the national condiment — served with couscous, stirred into soups, smeared onto bread. The word derives from the Arabic harasa, to pound, a reference to the traditional method of grinding dried chillies with spices using a mortar. Harissa spread from Tunisia across North Africa and into the Middle East and is now widely available in supermarkets in two main forms: rose harissa (milder, with rose petals and sweet pepper) and regular harissa (hotter, more intensely chilli-forward). Both are useful kitchen staples.
